column1: Vegetable combination (green beans, broccoli, onions, mushrooms)

column4: you can either manually copy or link the value from the "ingredients carbon footprint tab and table in this column, or copy the powerful and magic index-match formula from D5 or D8 - see explanation how it works below - could be life changing if you use excel regularly

column1: Use of Index-match to find automatically a cell in a range
The combination of the Index and match function in excel is a very powerful way to automatically find values in a reference table, that is not ranked in the same order than the table you are using
It is more reliable than the Vlookup or Hlookup functions which do not adapt automatically if you e.g. insert a column in your reference table afterwards
Step 1: return the value at a certain poition in a refrence table
Index($value_range,n) return the value of the nth cell in $value_range (that must be blocked with $ since you want to keep it fixed when you copy)
If you look at cell D8,

column4: with n= match(…) that we will learn below

column1: it returns the nth cell in the range 'Ingredients carbon footprint'!$F$2:F$11, that t is the range $F$2:F$11 from the Ingredients carbon footprint tab. That is 0.84 kgCO2/kg

which gives you what you want, i.e Loss-Adjusted Carbon footprint
(kg CO2eq/kg consumed)
Step 2: identify the rank n of this nth cell in the reference table.
This is where we use the match function
MATCH(lookup_value, $lookup_range,0) returns the relative position of the lookup_value in the $lookup_range
Taking cell D8 as an example, MATCH('Carbon footprint'!A8,'Ingredients carbon footprint'!$A$2:$A$11,0) looks for the position of "tortilla" in the lookup

column3: because "tortilla" as defined by Carbon footprint'!A8 is the third item in the table.

column1: Again, you need to block the lookup range with $ so that it does not cheng when you copy it
You also add " ,0) " at the end of the match function that indicates that you want an exact match
Thus =INDEX('Ingredients carbon footprint'!$F$2:F$11,3) returns the 3rd value in that table , which is 0.96
When you copy this cell o the other of column D, you get automatically the right values --> saves a lot of times and mistakes with larger tables!
Index can also work with an array instead of a single column range, then using two match function, one to identify the column and one to identify the row
This isused in e.g. cell C7 of the nutritional value , but no need to copy these
